description: NFS is used to share files over a network, mainly in Unix and Linux environments. In labs and CTFs, NFS can expose exported directories, readable files, writable shares, backups, and user or permission clues.
why_prioritize: NFS can expose entire directories without normal interactive login.
Readable exports may contain credentials, SSH keys, source code, backups, or configuration files.
Writable exports can reveal important permission behavior and may connect to other services in the lab.

enumeration_steps.title: List exported directories
enumeration_steps.reason: Exports show which directories are shared and who is allowed to mount them.

enumeration_steps.title: Check NFS-related RPC services
enumeration_steps.reason: NFS often depends on RPC services such as mountd, nlockmgr, and rpcbind.

enumeration_steps.title: Mount an export for review
enumeration_steps.reason: Mounting an allowed export lets you inspect files and permissions like a normal directory.

enumeration_steps.title: Review file ownership and permissions
enumeration_steps.reason: NFS permissions depend heavily on UIDs, GIDs, and export options, so ownership can reveal useful context.

next_steps: Look for SSH keys, shell history files, notes, and configuration files.
Check file ownership and usernames connected to the exported path.
Search readable files for passwords, hostnames, and internal paths.
Use discovered usernames when checking SSH or other login services.
Mount the export from your lab machine if allowed.
Review permissions and exported content carefully.
Check whether the export is read-only or writable.
Document the exact export path and allowed clients.
Download copies for offline review.
Search for passwords, tokens, database settings, internal paths, and usernames.
Check whether discovered credentials work on related services in scope.
Look for application names and versions mentioned inside the files.
Confirm write access with a harmless test file.
Check whether files are created with your UID or mapped to another user.
Look for links between the export and web directories, scripts, or backups.
Remove test files after checking to keep the lab clean.
Identify the likely username from path names, file ownership, or comments.
Copy the key carefully and set restrictive permissions before testing it.
Try the key only against systems inside the authorized lab scope.
If the key requires a passphrase, continue searching files for related clues.

beginner_notes: Always check showmount output when NFS is open.
Use ls -lan to see numeric UIDs and GIDs because names may not match your local system.
Unmount exports after testing so your lab directory stays clean.
NFS findings are often file-based, so read files carefully instead of rushing.
